key_insight: The paper separates microscopy into a sample twin and an instrument twin, then couples them to predict outcomes, uncertainty, and risk before acting.
engineering_relevance: This provides a practical framework for autonomous microscopy planning, helping operators and AI choose safer, more informative actions in closed-loop experiments.
method: Physics-informed encoder reconstructs scanner-driving descriptors from force-distance curves.
Deterministic scanner model simulates cantilever and feedback dynamics with sparse learned residual corrections.
Coupled twins jointly forecast expected measurement outcomes and operating risk for candidate microscope operations.
evidence_signal: In amplitude-modulation scanning probe microscopy, the encoder recovered descriptors with sub-nanometer accuracy and the calibrated scanner reproduced typical traces within a few nanometers, with mismatch traced mainly to operating-point noise amplification and phase-channel residuals.
watch_item: Performance depends on accurate calibration of both twins, and residual errors in the phase channel suggest that missing physics can still limit predictive fidelity.
paper_title: From Closed-Loop Optimization to Open Decision Making: Coupled Digital Twins for Predictive and Autonomous Microscopy
